root_text.an3.96:1.1: “Tīhi, bhikkhave, aṅgehi samannāgato rañño bhadro assājānīyo rājāraho hoti rājabhoggo, rañño aṅganteva saṅkhyaṁ gacchati.
root_text.an3.96:1.3: Idha, bhikkhave, rañño bhadro assājānīyo vaṇṇasampanno ca hoti balasampanno ca javasampanno ca.
root_text.an3.96:1.4: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, tīhi aṅgehi samannāgato rañño bhadro assājānīyo rājāraho hoti rājabhoggo, rañño aṅganteva saṅkhyaṁ gacchati.
root_text.an3.96:1.5: Evamevaṁ kho, bhikkhave, tīhi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āhuneyyo hoti pāhuneyyo dakkhiṇeyyo añjalikaraṇīyo anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassa.
root_text.an3.96:1.7: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vaṇṇasampanno ca hoti balasampanno ca javasampanno ca.
root_text.an3.96:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sīlavā hoti, pātimokkhasaṁvarasaṁvuto viharati ācāragocarasampanno aṇumattesu vajjesu bhayadassāvī, samādāya sikkhati sikkhāpadesu.
root_text.an3.96:2.3: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu vaṇṇasampanno hoti.
root_text.an3.96:3.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu āraddhavīriyo viharati akusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ pahānāya kusalānaṁ dhammānaṁ upasampadāya thāmavā daḷhaparakkamo anikkhittadhuro kusalesu dhammesu.
root_text.an3.96:3.3: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu balasampanno hoti.
root_text.an3.96:4.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu ‘idaṁ dukkhan’ti yathābhūtaṁ pajānāti;
root_text.an3.96:4.6: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu javasampanno hoti.
root_text.an3.96:4.7: Imehi kho, bhikkhave, tīhi dhammehi samannāgato bhikkhu āhuneyyo hoti pāhuneyyo dakkhiṇeyyo añjalikaraṇīyo anuttaraṁ puññakkhettaṁ lokassā”ti.

translation_text.an3.96:1.1: “Mendicants, a fine royal thoroughbred with three factors is worthy of a king, fit to serve a king, and reckoned as a factor of kingship.
translation_text.an3.96:1.3: It’s when a fine royal thoroughbred is beautiful, strong, and fast.
translation_text.an3.96:1.4: A fine royal thoroughbred with these three factors is worthy of a king. …
translation_text.an3.96:1.5: In the same way, a mendicant with three qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with joined palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.
translation_text.an3.96:1.7: It’s when a mendicant is beautiful, strong, and fast.
translation_text.an3.96:2.1: And how is a mendicant beautiful?
translation_text.an3.96:2.2: It’s when a mendicant is ethical, restrained in the monastic code, conducting themselves well and resorting for alms in suitable places. Seeing danger in the slightest fault, they keep the rules they’ve undertaken.
translation_text.an3.96:2.3: That’s how a mendicant is beautiful.
translation_text.an3.96:3.1: And how is a mendicant strong?
translation_text.an3.96:3.2: It’s when a mendicant lives with energy roused up for giving up unskillful qualities and embracing skillful qualities. They are strong, staunchly vigorous, not slacking off when it comes to developing skillful qualities.
translation_text.an3.96:3.3: That’s how a mendicant is strong.
translation_text.an3.96:4.1: And how is a mendicant fast?
translation_text.an3.96:4.2: It’s when a mendicant truly understands: ‘This is suffering’ …
translation_text.an3.96:4.5: ‘This is the practice that leads to the cessation of suffering’.
translation_text.an3.96:4.6: That’s how a mendicant is fast.
translation_text.an3.96:4.7: A mendicant with these three qualities is worthy of offerings dedicated to the gods, worthy of hospitality, worthy of a religious donation, worthy of veneration with joined palms, and is the supreme field of merit for the world.”

variant_text.an3.96:1.1: bhadro → bhaddo (sya-all, pts1ed, mr) | saṅkhyaṁ → saṅkhaṁ (bj, sya-all, km, pts1ed)
